skill.name: performance-optimization
skill.description: Guides measurement-first application performance improvements across React rendering, bundle size, images, database queries, caching, and Web Vitals.
skill.summary: Improve web application speed with profiling, React optimization, bundle analysis, image handling, database tuning, and caching.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 51 alerts, but manual review found no malicious intent or executable payload. Dynamic imports, network URLs, relative paths, and backtick alerts are documentation examples or markdown syntax in SKILL.md. The only residual risk is that users may choose to run documented CLI commands against their own projects.

low_findings.title: Documented CLI Commands Require Normal Developer Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for installing and running Lighthouse and webpack-bundle-analyzer. These are legitimate performance tools, but users should review commands before executing them in a project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear developer tooling examples, not hidden execution logic. Residual risk exists only if a user or assistant runs them without normal review.

dangerous_patterns.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Dynamic Import Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import alerts are React lazy-loading and code-splitting examples. They import local page components or chart.js on user interaction and do not load remote code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context labels these snippets as lazy loading and dynamic imports for bundle optimization. No user-controlled module path or remote import source is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Markdown Backticks Detected As Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by fenced code blocks, inline tags, and a Redis template literal in documentation. No Ruby code or shell interpolation is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these lines are markdown fences or TypeScript examples. There is no executable skill script using Ruby backticks or shell command substitution.

dangerous_patterns.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hardcoded URLs Are Examples And References
dangerous_patterns.description: The hardcoded URLs are a placeholder Lighthouse target and public documentation references. They do not receive secrets, telemetry, or project data from the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to example.com, web.dev, react.dev, and a GitHub project reference. No code path sends credentials or private data to these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Relative Related-Skill Links
dangerous_patterns.description: The path traversal alerts are markdown links to neighboring skill documentation. They are not file reads, file writes, or runtime path construction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reported lines are under a Related skills heading and contain static markdown links. No filesystem API or user-controlled path handling is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak Cryptography Detector Match
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported weak cryptography locations do not contain cryptographic code. Line 3 is the skill description, and line 296 is a related-skill link.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage at the reported locations. This appears to be a text-pattern mismatch.

content.value_statement: Slow pages, heavy bundles, and inefficient queries reduce user satisfaction and conversion. This skill guides measurement, targeted fixes, and validation across frontend and backend performance work.
actual_capabilities: Creates performance checklists for frontend, backend, and measurement work.
Suggests React memoization, callback stabilization, lazy loading, and code splitting patterns.
Recommends bundle analysis, tree shaking, and dynamic import opportunities.
Guides image optimization using responsive sizing, WebP, lazy loading, and priority handling.
Identifies database query issues such as N+1 queries and missing indexes.
Recommends cache usage and response optimization practices for common web applications.
limitations: It does not run benchmarks unless the host tool has project and command access.
It cannot guarantee performance gains without real profiling data.
Database advice must be adapted to the specific database engine and workload.
Some examples assume React, Next.js, Redis, Lighthouse, or webpack-based tooling.

use_cases.title: Improve React Rendering
use_cases.description: Find unnecessary re-renders and choose targeted memoization or lazy loading fixes.

use_cases.title: Reduce Production Bundle Size
use_cases.description: Audit heavy dependencies, split routes, and create a practical bundle reduction plan.

use_cases.title: Tune Data Access
use_cases.description: Review slow queries, N+1 patterns, indexes, and caching choices for user-facing endpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Performance Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my application symptoms and create a prioritized performance checklist. Focus on measurement first, then frontend, backend, and monitoring actions.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze React Slowdowns
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this React component or page for unnecessary re-renders. Recommend specific changes using memoization, stable callbacks, lazy loading, or simpler rendering.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Bundle Size Reduction
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my dependency list and bundle analysis notes. Identify likely bundle size wins, code splitting points, and safe tree-shaking improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Design Full Performance Remediation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a complete performance remediation plan from these Web Vitals, query timings, bundle data, and user complaints. Include validation steps and rollback risks.

output_examples.input: A React dashboard has poor Lighthouse scores and a slow first load.
output: Start with Lighthouse and Web Vitals measurements for LCP, CLS, and interaction latency.
Split dashboard-only routes and lazy load the heaviest chart components.
Audit image priority and responsive sizes for the largest visible content.
Use bundle analysis to find oversized dependencies before replacing libraries.

output_examples.input: A product listing endpoint becomes slow when many products are displayed.
output: Check for N+1 query patterns when loading product, category, and inventory data.
Add or validate indexes for filters, sorting fields, and join keys.
Cache stable product metadata when freshness requirements allow it.
Measure query time before and after each change to avoid speculative optimization.

best_practices: Measure the bottleneck before changing code.
Apply one optimization at a time and compare before-and-after metrics.
Track Web Vitals and key backend timings continuously after release.
anti_patterns: Do not add memoization everywhere without evidence of re-render cost.
Do not replace readable code with complex optimizations for minor gains.
Do not rely on synthetic scores alone when real user metrics are available.

faq.question: Does this skill run Lighthouse automatically?
faq.answer: No. It describes how to use Lighthouse, but command execution depends on the host tool and user approval.

faq.question: Is this only for React applications?
faq.answer: No. It includes React examples, but it also covers bundles, images, database queries, caching, and measurement.

faq.question: Can it fix database performance issues?
faq.answer: It can suggest query, index, and caching improvements. Final changes require database-specific review and testing.

faq.question: Does it replace profiling tools?
faq.answer: No. It works best with data from Lighthouse, Web Vitals, query plans, bundle analyzers, and production monitoring.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I provide for best results?
faq.answer: Provide performance symptoms, metrics, relevant code, bundle reports, slow query examples, and target user flows.
